narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 40% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The key factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your planned part-time enrollment status, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students opt for part-time compared to over 92% full-time—and the model sees this as a notable risk for bar success based on past patterns. On a more positive note, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 6 was slightly above the average of 5.53, which helped pull the prediction toward passing by showing solid mid-program performance. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 8—well above the average 5.71—strongly supported a passing outcome, highlighting your strong start academically. Your LSAT score of 30 falls below the typical 36.70 average, and middle family income aligns with the norm, but these played lesser roles compared to the top influences. Overall, while your early grades stand out favorably against most applicants, the part-time choice outweighed them, tipping the balance toward a failure risk in the model's eyes. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, just how these factors stacked up for someone in your situation. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time commitment, as that alone could shift the prediction significantly. Boosting your LSAT through targeted prep might also help, given its common importance. Retaking undergrad-level courses or gaining relevant experience could strengthen your decile rankings further. Finally, reaching out to admissions for feedback or exploring programs with flexible enrollment could open new paths tailored to your circumstances.
